steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb? (appears twice)

hints.deponendam: 'deponendam' is a gerundive (verbal adjective), not a finite verb
hints.audaciam: 'audaciam' is a noun in the accusative case

hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'vixisti' — 'vivis' is present tense
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'vivas' — 'vivis' is indicative

hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vito, vitare, vitavi, vitatum: These are the parts of 'vitare' (to avoid)

reveal.gloss: to be laid aside / for laying aside

hints.Present participle: Present participle would be 'deponens' — 'deponendam' with -ndam ending is a gerundive
hints.Perfect participle: Perfect participle would be 'depositam' — 'deponendam' is a gerundive expressing purpose
hints.Future active participle: Future active participle would be 'depositura' — 'deponendam' is a gerundive

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'audacia' — 'audaciam' with -am ending is accusative
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'audacia' — 'audaciam' is accusative, governed by 'ad'

hints.2nd: 2nd declension accusative ends in -um — 'audacia, -ae' is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension accusative ends in -em — 'audacia, -ae' is 1st declension
hints.5th: 5th declension accusative ends in -em — 'audacia, -ae' is 1st declension

steps.prompt: What does 'ad deponendam audaciam' express grammatically?
steps.target: Purpose — 'in order to lay aside audacity'
choices: Purpose — 'in order to lay aside audacity'
Result — 'so that audacity was laid aside'

hints.Cause — 'because of audacity': 'ad' + gerundive expresses purpose, not cause — cause would use 'propter' or an ablative
hints.Result — 'so that audacity was laid asid: Result uses 'ut' + subjunctive — 'ad' + gerundive is a purpose construction
hints.Time — 'when audacity was laid aside': Temporal clauses use 'cum' or ablative absolute — 'ad' + gerundive expresses purpose

steps.prompt: What is the rhetorical force of repeating 'vivis ... vivis'?
steps.target: Epizeuxis — repetition for emphasis, stressing Catiline's continued survival
choices: Epizeuxis — repetition for emphasis, stressing Catiline's continued survival

hints.Chiasmus — reversed word order: Chiasmus reverses parallel elements (ABBA) — this is simple repetition of the same word
hints.Litotes — understatement: Litotes negates the opposite — this is emphatic repetition, the opposite of understatement
hints.Hyperbaton — displaced word order: Hyperbaton involves unusual word placement — the key figure here is the repetition itself

translation: You live, and you live not to lay aside your audacity.
